item.title: Could use a general revision/rewrite.
item.content:encoded: An interesting idea but I'm in the same boat as many others in this article, there are just too many small details and writing issues about this SCP that just make it feel underwhelming. Perhaps its just personal opinion, but this is one of the few popular posts I dislike.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The whole "Area 51 thing is actually real" is a concept with a lot of potential but I feel like this SCP really doesn't do much with it…

Bit bland :/

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the first image has been removed as it comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Intruders into this radius are to be shot. Personnel are reminded to not injure intruders, but only shoot a sufficient amount to repel them.

"Shooting (at) someone a sufficient amount to repel them" sounds very silly. Also, you don't shoot someone and manage to not injure them. I think the first sentence should end, "Are to be shot at."

How about saying, "Intruders approaching within this radius are to be repelled by warning shots. Personnel are reminded not to injure intruders." Sorry to reword so much, but "Intruders into this radius" isn't great phrasing either.

SCP-1051 has remained in appearance to an aircraft hangar.

I think this was meant to read, "SCP-1051 has remained similar in appearance to an aircraft hangar."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I didn't thought of that. It makes sense!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not the author. But in general, square brackets in interviews, quotes and the like would imply that the speaker did not say that originally in the interview, but they are added in (by the editor) so that other people will know the context.

e.g.

Interviewer: What is your opinion of the exams?

Me: Difficult.

If I choose to quote only 'Difficult', readers might not know the context behind the word. Thus, I would add square brackets to establish context – '[The exams are] difficult'.

Having said that, it is best to ask the author directly. Perhaps PM if they're not responding here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are the square brackets around "Dr. James" really needed? I guess they are just for the temporary space of the doctor's name, left unfinished…

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, so here we have Area-51, as an alien, what shoots out UFO eggs. Thats good stuff. One area that could lead to a potentially easy and non-invasive crosslink is the mention of Men in Black conspiracy theories in the second addenda. Since we do have an entire GoI that is a psuedo-men in black group, aka the UIU. I was thinking a simple link to their GoI hub. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Various "Men in Black" theories of extra-terrestrial contact

To this:

Various "Men in Black" theories of extra-terrestrial contact

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: Area 51 as a SCP? Genius!
item.content:encoded:
This always amuses me that the SCPs that are well known in Conspiracy Pop Culture (Area 51, Flying Saucers, Bigfoot), is actually something we didn't expect. Whoever came up with this idea, was brilliant.

Still, Area 51 is an alien? That would take the conspiracy nuts completely by surprise (no offense to those who do believe in them).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I tried to upvote this three separate times. I'll agree something was off about the dialogue in interview 2, but otherwise, it's a great concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That dialog is… not good at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked how it tied into real world events. This is now my favorite SCP. Except for my own of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Somewhat of a necropost, but agreeing. The core concept is really, really nifty, but the dialogue sounds so stilted and wooden that it completely killed my enjoyment of the article. The second interview doesn't even seem necessary and could probably be shortened to a foreword for the third interview or something. Neutral voting.

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: The second image in this article is a modification of a satellite image, used here with the permission of the original's owners, DigitalGlobe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want this to work for me, but, the dialog, oh god, the dialog. The dialog in this is absolutely horrid, and ruins the entire SCP for me. Human beings do not talk like that, and if you ever encounter one that does, congratulations, you've found an alien!

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it! Dionaea House + UFO + pettiness = great SCP.

However, the interviews, especially the last one, while important to the story, are kind of… not good. Still, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The second interview… I don't like it because the interviewer mostly asks leading questions ("I know where I want this to go. I'll just hurry it along…") Also, if it was changed in style but not substance, it should be first.

Closing Statement: I'll have to have a chat with this Dr. Gold. I have a hunch. - Dr. Richardson

That's… There is nothing in that line that should not be removed.

Oh, and hire Dr. Gold afterwards. He seems like the type.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
dressed in uniform superficially resembling that of the United States Air Force

Why not get some actual USAF uniform?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry, but I just don't like this. The interview logs seems really shallow, for some reason.

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I KNEW IT

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For those curious as to the actual physical make-up of the SCP:

[16:41] Tanhony can you confirm that 1051 is a giant reality bending cat then
[16:41] Salmander Yes
[16:41] Salmander Yes it is

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Spectacular. This needs to win.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got this bro

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You missed an "air-force". Mind if I get that for you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this and upvoted it. Very clever.

One thing, the phrasing in your interview log was kind of awkward. May want to smooth out the dialogue a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm… I prefer the other UFO on the site, but this isn't bad. Need to mull on this…

These are pretty much my thoughts, but this works well enough for an upvote, for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hah, well, fair enough, then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1000 has a shell that mimics large structures.

I guess this was kind of implied along with what was testified in the interview logs. Since it's the only shape the thing has held, I guess there's no real way of knowing, but it would be pretty improbable for a creature from another world to look just like a specific engineered design from our world (THAT'S RIGHT TRANSFORMERS I'M CALLING YOU OUT).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This, like a few other entries in this contest, was way better then I imagined it would be when I saw the picture. The one thing that still bothers me- which maybe you answered in the text, but maybe not- was, does the adult just happen to look like a hangar? I understand there are alternatives that might lead to its 'camouflage', but I don't know what any of them are.
…Of course, if you did address this, feel free to correct me. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty interesting. I can't believe I didn't think of doing aliens. Not that I'm unhappy with mine, but it should have been an obvious idea. Anyways, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kind of like this. It seems a bit harmless for a Keter, but no matter. Just a nifty thing. See, now if the Foundation were smart, they'd track where the eggs go. This thing has to be shooting them at occupied worlds, or else it'll just die out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm… I prefer the other UFO on the site, but this isn't bad. Need to mull on this…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, nice catch. Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Missed something in the proofreading. It's "United States Air Force", or "USAF", not "United States air-force".

This does make it a little scary that in the '80s, the place colloquially known as "Area 51" was one of the places we were sending nuclear targeting data for the SIOP…

(Out here in our reality, of course, that's because the place now known as Homey AFB was a testing ground for experimental aircraft, and served as the sole base for the B-2 until they were first deployed to Montana. In the Foundation universe, however…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you to Dexanote for original idea and Thedeadlymoose for proof-reading and stuff. Hell, small shout-out to TaNhony for joining the brain-storming crew. All glory to the brochat.
